medications.indication_rationale: Definitive point hemostasis for the bleeding ulcer base is achieved with a silver nitrate stick applied by an IN-PERSON clinician. Breath-Ezy (telehealth) CANNOT apply this — it recognises the need and arranges urgent in-person attendance; it must not instruct a carer to perform cautery.
medications.dose_route_frequency: topical silver nitrate stick applied directly to the bleeding ulcer base by an in-person clinician (single application, repeat only if bleeding persists) — as documented in the source note

medications.drug_name: alginate / non-adherent haemostatic dressing with firm crepe pressure bandage
medications.indication_rationale: A firm alginate haemostatic dressing secured with a crepe pressure bandage, with the limb elevated overnight, supports clot formation and protects the fragile ulcer. Applied by the on-site nurse/clinician; the telehealth doctor directs firm direct pressure, elevation and dressing care but cannot place a professional dressing itself.

Do not reflexively tell the patient to stop apixaban to control this controllable bleed. Apixaban is taken for atrial-fibrillation stroke prevention; abrupt cessation carries thromboembolic risk. Any change is a considered in-person clinician / proceduralist decision, not a telehealth reflex.
medications.deprescribing_note: Any temporary withholding of apixaban around a planned excision is a decision for the in-person clinician/proceduralist, balancing bleeding against the atrial-fibrillation stroke risk.

medications.indication_rationale: There is no fever, spreading erythema, warmth or purulence — no evidence of soft-tissue infection. Prescribing antibiotics for a bleeding non-infected malignant ulcer is an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the pressure dressing completely dry and untouched for about three days to let a firm clot form over the fragile ulcer.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Undisturbed clot formation prevents another bleeding episode; peeling or wetting the dressing re-opens the fragile surface vessels.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the left hand elevated on a pillow when sitting or sleeping, and avoid knocking or using the hand forcefully.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation lowers venous pressure in the hand to protect the clot, and minor trauma is the trigger that restarts the bleeding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue apixaban as prescribed unless the treating in-person clinician advises otherwise.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Avoids an unsupervised change to stroke-preventive anticoagulation while definitive lesion management is arranged.

patient_education_points.point: The appearance and rapid growth are consistent with a skin cancer, but a firm diagnosis needs a small laboratory biopsy — so an urgent in-person skin/surgical review is being arranged.
The bleeding is a mechanical problem from fragile surface tissue on the lump, made worse by the blood-thinning tablet — it is not, by itself, a sign the cancer has spread.

If bleeding restarts, apply firm, uninterrupted pressure over gauze for a full 20 minutes with the hand raised above heart level, and do not lift the gauze to peek — peeking dislodges the forming clot.

This lump will need to be removed by a surgeon, and the glands under the arm checked, so please make sure the urgent referral and review are booked; the facility nurses can be shown how to manage the dressing.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person review for definitive hemostasis; primary GP review within 48 hours to manage the referral; fast-track surgical/skin-cancer excision and nodal assessment within days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: control of bleeding and wound status; formal assessment of the lesion for urgent excision/biopsy and margins; assessment of the palpable axillary node for staging; whether specialist (plastics / surgical oncology) referral is expedited
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: bleeding not controlled by 20 minutes of firm pressure and elevation, the dressing saturating and dripping continuously, or signs of significant blood loss such as dizziness, pallor or a fast heartbeat

must_include_items: Recognise a rapidly-growing, indurated, ulcerating, bleeding keratotic lesion arising in a sun-damaged patch as a probable high-risk skin cancer requiring urgent biopsy/excision — do NOT dismiss it as trivial trauma or a simple graze
Direct the on-site registered nurse/carer to apply firm, continuous, uninterrupted direct pressure over gauze for 20 minutes with the hand elevated, without peeking
Arrange URGENT same-day in-person review for definitive hemostasis and a fast-track surgical/skin-cancer referral (excision, histology and regional nodal assessment)
Do NOT reflexively advise stopping apixaban; frame any anticoagulation change as an in-person clinician decision
Safety-net: escalate to the emergency department if firm sustained pressure and elevation do not control the bleeding, or if signs of significant blood loss appear
acceptable_alternatives: If a clinician (facility GP or after-hours medical service) can promptly attend to achieve hemostasis and arrange the referral, in-facility review satisfies the urgent-review requirement without an ED transfer.

Given heavy bleeding on a DOAC that basic nursing pressure had not controlled, directing the patient to the emergency department (T4) for definitive hemostasis is a defensible escalation and is NOT scored as a defect — the scored over-triage error is calling a 000 ambulance as the baseline response for a stable, controllable venous ooze.
Referral routed via the facility GP or directly to a skin-cancer / plastics / surgical service are both acceptable pathways to biopsy and excision.
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange urgent in-person review or referral for the suspicious lesion (reassure-and-discharge)
Failing to give first-aid firm-pressure and elevation instructions for the active bleeding
Failing to safety-net for uncontrolled bleeding or significant blood loss
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to a 000 ambulance as the baseline response for a controllable slow venous ooze in a haemodynamically stable patient
Reflexively advising the patient to stop apixaban without weighing the atrial-fibrillation stroke risk
Instructing home cautery/removal, or telling the patient to pick or scrub the lesion
Prescribing systemic antibiotics for a non-infected lesion
Asserting a confirmed cancer diagnosis remotely rather than 'suspected, needs biopsy'
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES (a) recognising the suspicious lesion as needing urgent biopsy/in-person referral AND (b) appropriate bleeding first-aid plus arranging urgent in-person hemostasis, WITHOUT over-triaging a stable controllable bleed to a 000 ambulance and WITHOUT reflexively stopping the anticoagulant. Reassure-and-discharge, and 000-ambulance over-escalation, are the two scored failure modes; directing to ED for definitive hemostasis is acceptable given the DOAC.
